CONTENT: 1️⃣ Code Hygiene
Resolve all compilation warnings
SA1200 – ensure using directives are alphabetised
SA1028 – ensure closing curly brace } is on its own line
SA1101 – ensure each method is documented with appropriate XML comments
2️⃣ Unit Testing
Add relevant builders using the builder pattern for complex test objects
Ensure builders create objects that are easy to modify and extend for test scenarios
Ensure each Action has unit tests covering Request / Handler / Behaviour
Test the Request – validate input to the handler
Test the Handler – validate the handler logic
Test Behaviour – validate logging / transactions and other side effects
Ensure each Action has a Validator test covering all input validation rules
Follow the unit test naming convention: MethodName_StateUnderTest_ExpectedResult
3️⃣ Code Structure
Ensure each Action folder contains Request / Response / Handler / Validator
Request – represents the data passed into the Action
Response – represents the data returned after processing
Handler – contains the logic that processes the Action
Validator – ensures input is valid before the Action is processed
4️⃣ Entity Framework
Ensure validation constraints align with EF entity model constraints (e.g. max-length)
Ensure AsNoTracking() is used for read-only queries
Review entities to identify fields that would benefit from an index
5️⃣ General Best Practices
Leverage Global Usings to reduce redundant file-level using statements
Ensure DTOs follow the MapToDto naming convention (e.g. UserMapToDto)
Ensure the Handler implements validation logic (e.g. via FluentValidation)
Ensure the Handler inherits from BaseHandler
6️⃣ General Structure & Readability
Is the code easy to understand without excessive comments?
Are functions and methods named clearly and concisely?
Are complex or nested blocks refactored for clarity?
Are files and classes named using .NET PascalCase conventions?
Does the code use a consistent indentation style throughout?
7️⃣ DRY – Don't Repeat Yourself
Is there any duplicate code that can be extracted into a reusable method or class?
Can any logic be abstracted into reusable functions or components?
Are magic numbers or strings replaced with named constants or enums?
Are utility methods or extension methods used where appropriate?
8️⃣ Method Design
Are methods small and focused (Single Responsibility Principle)?
Are methods generally under 20–30 lines in length?
Are method signatures clear and avoiding excessive parameters?
Are methods and classes loosely coupled to facilitate testing?
9️⃣ Naming Conventions
Are classes / methods / properties using PascalCase?
Are local variables and parameters using camelCase?
Are names meaningful and descriptive – avoiding unclear abbreviations?
🔟 Clean Code & SOLID Principles
S – Single Responsibility Principle: each class has one reason to change
O – Open/Closed Principle: open for extension; closed for modification
L – Liskov Substitution Principle: subtypes are substitutable for base types
I – Interface Segregation Principle: no class depends on methods it does not use
D – Dependency Inversion Principle: depend on abstractions not concretions
Are hardcoded values replaced with constants or configuration?
Are exceptions handled correctly and not swallowed silently?
Are async/await patterns used correctly (no Task.Wait() or Task.Result blocking calls)?
1️⃣1️⃣ Testing & Testability
Is there sufficient unit test coverage including boundary and edge cases?
Are test names clear and descriptive?
Are tests isolated and independent of each other?
Are external services and databases mocked or stubbed in unit tests?
1️⃣2️⃣ Error Handling & Logging
Are appropriate exception types caught with meaningful messages?
Does logging follow a consistent format?
Is sensitive data (e.g. passwords) excluded from log output?
1️⃣3️⃣ Performance Considerations
Are there obvious performance bottlenecks such as expensive operations in loops?
Is the code making efficient use of resources (no memory or connection leaks)?
Are appropriate collections and data structures chosen for the task?
